section.title: Manna and Quail
section.sectionRationale: Hunger, not thirst, is the next test of the wilderness, and God answers it with a daily miracle that teaches Israel dependence, obedience, and the meaning of the Sabbath rest, all at once. Jesus later takes up this very chapter to explain who He is.
section.thesis: When Israel grumbles for food, the LORD rains down bread from heaven and sends quail, testing whether His people will gather only what they need, trust Him for the seventh day without gathering, and remember, for every generation to come, the bread He gave them in the wilderness.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Israel's grumbling for the food of Egypt; God's promise of bread and meat and the reason for it; the manna and quail actually given, gathered, and mismanaged; the Sabbath test of trust; and the jar of manna kept as a lasting memorial.

units.label: Grumbling in the desert
units.note: we sat by pots of meat

verses.text: On the fifteenth day of the second month after they had left the land of Egypt, the whole congregation of Israel set out from Elim and came to the Desert of Sin, which is between Elim and Sinai.
And there in the desert they all grumbled against Moses and Aaron.

“If only we had died by the LORD's hand in the land of Egypt!” they said. “There we sat by pots of meat and ate our fill of bread, but you have brought us into this desert to starve this whole assembly to death!”

Then the LORD said to Moses, “Behold, I will rain down bread from heaven for you. Each day the people are to go out and gather enough for that day. In this way I will test whether or not they will follow My instructions.
Then on the sixth day, when they prepare what they bring in, it will be twice as much as they gather on the other days.”
So Moses and Aaron said to all the Israelites, “This evening you will know that it was the LORD who brought you out of the land of Egypt,
and in the morning you will see the LORD's glory, because He has heard your grumbling against Him. For who are we that you should grumble against us?”

And Moses added, “The LORD will give you meat to eat this evening and bread to fill you in the morning, for He has heard your grumbling against Him. Who are we? Your grumblings are not against us but against the LORD.”
Then Moses said to Aaron, “Tell the whole congregation of Israel, ‘Come before the LORD, for He has heard your grumbling.’”
And as Aaron was speaking to the whole congregation of Israel, they looked toward the desert, and there in a cloud the glory of the LORD appeared.
Then the LORD said to Moses,
“I have heard the grumbling of the Israelites. Tell them, ‘At twilight you will eat meat, and in the morning you will be filled with bread. Then you will know that I am the LORD your God.’”

units.label: Manna and quail given
units.note: what is it? and gathering according to need

media.alt: Thin flakes on the desert floor at dawn
media.caption: Bread from heaven, given fresh every morning.

verses.text: That evening quail came and covered the camp, and in the morning there was a layer of dew around the camp.
When the layer of dew had evaporated, there were thin flakes on the desert floor, as fine as frost on the ground.

When the Israelites saw it, they asked one another, “What is it?” For they did not know what it was. 
So Moses told them, “It is the bread that the LORD has given you to eat.
This is what the LORD has commanded: ‘Each one is to gather as much as he needs. You may take an {{omer|omer}} for each person in your tent.’”
So the Israelites did this. Some gathered more, and some less.
When they measured it by the omer, he who gathered much had no excess, and he who gathered little had no shortfall. Each one gathered as much as he needed to eat.
Then Moses said to them, “No one may keep any of it until morning.”
But they did not listen to Moses; some people left part of it until morning, and it became infested with maggots and began to smell. So Moses was angry with them.
Every morning each one gathered as much as was needed, and when the sun grew hot, it melted away.

units.label: The Sabbath rest
units.note: a day of complete rest, a holy Sabbath

verses.text: On the sixth day, they gathered twice as much food, two omers per person, and all the leaders of the congregation came and reported this to Moses.

He told them, “This is what the LORD has said: ‘Tomorrow is to be a day of complete rest, a holy {{sabbath|Sabbath}} to the LORD. So bake what you want to bake, and boil what you want to boil. Then set aside whatever remains and keep it until morning.’”
So they set it aside until morning as Moses had commanded, and it did not smell or contain any maggots.
“Eat it today,” Moses said, “because today is a Sabbath to the LORD. Today you will not find anything in the field.
For six days you may gather, but on the seventh day, the Sabbath, it will not be there.”
Yet on the seventh day some of the people went out to gather, but they did not find anything.
Then the LORD said to Moses, “How long will you refuse to keep My commandments and instructions?
Understand that the LORD has given you the Sabbath; that is why on the sixth day He will give you bread for two days. On the seventh day, everyone must stay where he is; no one may leave his place.”
So the people rested on the seventh day.
Now the house of Israel called the bread manna. It was white like coriander seed and tasted like wafers made with honey.

Moses said, “This is what the LORD has commanded: ‘Keep an omer of manna for the generations to come, so that they may see the bread I fed you in the wilderness when I brought you out of the land of Egypt.’”
So Moses told Aaron, “Take a jar and fill it with an omer of manna. Then place it before the LORD to be preserved for the generations to come.”
And Aaron placed it in front of the Testimony, to be preserved just as the LORD had commanded Moses.
The Israelites ate manna forty years, until they came to a land where they could settle; they ate manna until they reached the border of Canaan.
(Now an omer is a tenth of an ephah.)

omer.gloss: A dry measure, roughly two liters, a tenth of an ephah. God commanded each person to gather exactly one omer, no more, no less, a built-in check on hoarding and a daily lesson in trusting Him for enough.

sabbath.gloss: Rest, cessation from labor. Introduced here even before the Sinai commandments, the Sabbath is tied directly to trusting God's provision, since no manna fell on the seventh day and yet the sixth day's gathering never spoiled.

basic.context: Six weeks after leaving Egypt, Israel runs out of food and grumbles that they were better off as slaves. God responds by raining down bread every morning and sending quail every evening, but He also builds in a daily test: gather only what you need, and trust Him enough to rest on the seventh day.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real, sustained forty-year provision for a real people in the wilderness. It is not primarily about food but about trust: whether Israel would obey God's instructions about gathering, hoarding, and resting, even when it seemed to make no practical sense.
basic.originalLanguages: Each person was to gather exactly one omer, a fixed measure that left no room for either shortage or greedy excess. The whole test turns on the shabbat, the rest, that God commanded on the seventh day, built into the very rhythm of daily bread long before it becomes a formal commandment at Sinai.
basic.typology: This bread from heaven, given daily and insufficient to hoard, anticipates Jesus' own description of Himself in John 6 as the true bread from heaven, given not just for a day but for eternal life, and His teaching His disciples to pray for daily bread.
basic.christ: In John 6:32-35, Jesus explicitly points back to this chapter and declares that He, not the manna, is the true bread from heaven, giving life to the world; whoever comes to Him will never hunger, fulfilling and surpassing what manna could only picture for a season.
basic.god: God hears grumbling and responds with generous provision rather than punishment, yet He also insists on obedience in how that provision is received, testing whether His people will trust Him daily rather than hoard for tomorrow. His care and His holiness are not in tension; both are expressed in the same provision.
basic.crossReferences: John 6:31-35 has Jesus identify Himself as the true bread from heaven that this manna foreshadowed. Hebrews 9:4 notes the golden jar of manna kept in the ark as a lasting testimony. Matthew 6:11 echoes this chapter's rhythm of daily bread in the Lord's Prayer.
basic.oneStory: God feeds a grumbling, undeserving people daily, testing whether they will trust Him rather than hoard or grasp for control. That pattern of daily, dependent trust in God's provision runs through the whole Bible and finds its true fulfillment in Christ, the bread from heaven who satisfies forever.

context.literary: The chapter's structure mirrors its lesson: grumbling, then provision, then instructions tested and initially disobeyed, then obedience achieved, closing with a memorial meant to teach every future generation the same lesson.
context.historical: This provision sustains Israel for the entire forty years of wilderness wandering, becoming, alongside the exodus and Sinai, one of the defining memories of Israel's formation as a covenant people utterly dependent on God.
context.canonical: The manna becomes a recurring biblical symbol of God's sustaining provision, invoked in the Psalms, referenced by Jesus in John 6, and echoed in the daily bread of the Lord's Prayer and the hidden manna promised to the overcomer in Revelation 2:17.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God provides generously even for a grumbling people, while using that provision to test and train Israel in daily trust, restrained gathering, and Sabbath rest even before the full law is given at Sinai.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific, unrepeated miracle of provision for Israel's forty-year wilderness journey; it does not prescribe that God will supernaturally provide food in this exact form for believers today. Its enduring principle, daily trust in God's provision and rest from anxious labor, is what the New Testament carries forward, not the literal manna itself.

debates.question: Why does the Sabbath appear here, before the Ten Commandments are given at Sinai?

views.label: The Sabbath as a creation ordinance
views.case: Since Genesis 2:2-3 already describes God resting on the seventh day, some see the Sabbath as woven into creation itself, simply made explicit and formalized for Israel here and later codified at Sinai.

views.label: The Sabbath introduced specifically for Israel here
views.case: Others note this is the first place a seventh-day rest is commanded of God's people directly, suggesting it functions primarily as a covenant sign given to Israel, later written into the Decalogue as a fixed, national ordinance.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage ties Sabbath rest to trust in God's provision rather than mere legal requirement, a connection carried into the fuller Sinai commandment and into later New Testament teaching on rest found in Christ, Hebrews 4:9-10.

study.originalLanguages: The fixed measure of one omer per person built a daily lesson in contentment into Israel's very survival, no reward for greed, no penalty for having less. And the command to shabbat, rest, on the seventh day, given here even before Sinai's formal law, ties the whole rhythm of provision to trust rather than mere labor.

typology.type: Bread rained down daily from heaven
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ, the true bread from heaven, John 6:32-35, who gives life to the world and satisfies forever, unlike manna which had to be gathered fresh each day.

typology.type: The jar of manna kept before the Testimony
typology.fulfillment: A lasting witness to God's provision, echoed in the hidden manna promised to the one who conquers, Revelation 2:17.

study.christ: Jesus takes this very chapter as His text in John 6, telling the crowd that Moses did not give them the true bread from heaven, His Father does, and that He Himself is that bread, given for the life of the world. Where manna satisfied hunger only for a day and had to be gathered again each morning, Christ satisfies forever; whoever comes to Him will never hunger again.
study.god: God is patient with grumbling, choosing to hear and provide rather than immediately judge, yet He remains holy, insisting on obedience in exactly how that provision is received and used. His generosity and His authority are inseparable; the same God who rains down bread also commands rest and consistent trust.

crossReferences.note: Jesus identifies Himself as the true bread from heaven that this chapter's manna foreshadowed.
Give us this day our daily bread echoes the daily, non-hoarded provision of manna.
Notes the golden jar of manna kept in the ark of the covenant as a lasting testimony.
Promises hidden manna to the one who conquers, a final fulfillment of this chapter's provision.

study.oneStory: God feeds an undeserving, grumbling people every single day, teaching them to trust Him rather than hoard, and to rest on the seventh day rather than strive. This becomes the pattern the whole Bible follows toward Christ, the true bread from heaven, who alone satisfies completely and gives the deeper rest that manna in the wilderness could only foreshadow.

sermon.title: Food in the Desert
sermon.why: Preached on Exodus 16:2-5, 27-35, Keller traces the manna's daily provision and the discipline of gathering only enough, drawing the line to Christ as the true bread from heaven who alone satisfies.

quiz.q: What did Israel complain about at the start of this chapter?

options.text: That they had no food and claimed they had eaten better as slaves in Egypt.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 record this exact complaint, longing for Egypt's pots of meat and bread.

options.text: That the water was too bitter to drink.
options.note: That complaint belongs to the previous chapter, at Marah, not this one about hunger.

options.text: That Moses had led them the wrong direction geographically.
options.note: Their complaint is specifically about food, not the route taken.

options.text: They made no complaint in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 2 to 3 record a clear and bitter complaint about food.

quiz.q: How much manna was each person commanded to gather, according to verse 16?

options.text: An omer per person, exactly as much as was needed.
options.note: This fixed measure prevented both shortage and hoarding, as verse 18 confirms.

options.text: As much as each family could carry.
options.note: The command specified a fixed measure per person, not an open-ended amount.

options.text: Enough to last an entire week at once.
options.note: They were to gather daily, except on the sixth day when a double portion was allowed for the Sabbath.

options.text: No specific amount was commanded.
options.note: The omer measure was an explicit command.

quiz.q: What happened when some people kept manna overnight against instructions?

options.text: It became infested with maggots and began to smell.
options.note: Verse 20 describes this consequence of disobedience directly.

options.text: It multiplied into an even larger supply.
options.note: The opposite happened; the leftover manna spoiled rather than multiplying.

options.text: Nothing happened; the manna kept perfectly well.
options.note: The text explicitly says it spoiled, unlike the Sabbath-eve portion which stayed good.

options.text: The manna turned into quail.
options.note: The text describes spoilage with maggots, not a transformation into quail.

quiz.q: What made the manna gathered on the sixth day different from other days?

options.text: A double portion was gathered, and it did not spoil overnight, unlike other days.
options.note: Verses 22 to 24 describe this special provision for the Sabbath.

options.text: It tasted different from the manna on other days.
options.note: The text notes the amount and its preservation, not a change in taste.

options.text: No manna at all fell on the sixth day.
options.note: Manna did fall on the sixth day, in double portion; none fell on the seventh.

options.text: There was no difference; every day's manna behaved identically.
options.note: The sixth day's portion was uniquely preserved overnight without spoiling.

quiz.q: Why was a jar of manna kept before the Testimony, according to verses 32 to 34?

options.text: So future generations could see the bread the LORD gave Israel in the wilderness.
options.note: This is the explicit stated purpose, a lasting memorial for generations to come.

options.text: As food reserved for Moses' personal use.
options.note: The stated purpose is a memorial witness, not personal provision for Moses.

options.text: Because the manna in the jar was somehow more nutritious.
options.note: No such claim is made; its purpose was purely memorial.

options.text: No reason is given for keeping the jar.
options.note: Verse 32 explicitly states the memorial purpose.

questions: Israel grumbled that slavery in Egypt looked better than freedom with hardship. When has present difficulty tempted you to romanticize a past you were actually glad to escape?

God gave manna one day at a time, with no way to store it up for tomorrow except by disobedience. Where in your life are you trying to hoard security that God is asking you to trust Him for daily instead?

The Sabbath rest was tied directly to trusting God's provision, not just following a rule. What would it look like for you to rest this week not out of obligation but out of genuine trust that God will provide even when you stop working?
Jesus said He is the true bread from heaven, and whoever comes to Him will never hunger. What are you currently looking to for the kind of lasting satisfaction only Christ can actually give?

The jar of manna was kept so later generations would remember God's provision. What tangible reminder helps you remember God's past faithfulness when you are tempted to doubt His care in a new season?

translationNotes.note: What is it? renders the Hebrew phrase man hu, which many scholars believe is the origin of the name manna itself, a fitting name for a food Israel could not otherwise identify or explain.
The description white like coriander seed and tasting like wafers made with honey gives one of the Bible's most concrete sensory descriptions of a miraculous provision.

The closing note that an omer is a tenth of an ephah functions as an explanatory aside for readers unfamiliar with the measure, suggesting the text was compiled for an audience some distance from the original event.
